mnemonics.title: LINKED LIST NODE STRUCTURE
mnemonics.content: DATA + NEXT (Remember: D.N. = Data Next, like 'Double N' - Two parts: Data and Next pointer)

mnemonics.title: DOUBLY LINKED LIST POINTERS
mnemonics.content: PREV + DATA + NEXT (Remember: P.D.N. = Please Don't Neglect - Previous, Data, Next pointers)

mnemonics.title: INSERTION AT BEGINNING
mnemonics.content: NEW->NEXT = HEAD; HEAD = NEW (Remember: NEW before HEAD - like putting a new book BEFORE the first book on a shelf)

mnemonics.title: TIME COMPLEXITY REMINDER
mnemonics.content: INSERT BEGIN O(1), DELETE BEGIN O(1), SEARCH O(n), INSERT END O(n) - BEGIN is FAST, END and SEARCH are SLOW

acronyms.expansion: Linked List - linear data structure with nodes connected via pointers
acronyms.usage: Used to declare linked list variables and discuss in code comments

acronyms.expansion: Singly Linked List - unidirectional list with one pointer per node
acronyms.usage: When specifying the type of linked list being implemented

acronyms.expansion: Doubly Linked List - bidirectional list with prev and next pointers
acronyms.usage: When implementing reversible traversal or efficient back navigation

acronyms.expansion: Circular Linked List - list where last node points back to first
acronyms.usage: In round-robin scheduling or cyclic implementations

acronyms.expansion: First node of a linked list
acronyms.usage: Starting point for all traversals and operations

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box divided into two sections: left for DATA (store value), right for NEXT (arrow pointing to next node). Use an arrow symbol → to show pointer direction.
Draw HEAD pointing to box A. Create new box NEW with data X. Draw arrow from NEW to A, then redirect HEAD arrow to NEW. Shows 'putting new person at front of line'.
Show three boxes: PREV → CURRENT → NEXT. Insert NEW between PREV and CURRENT. First draw NEW→NEXT pointing to CURRENT, then PREV→NEW. Order matters!
Show PREV → TO_DELETE → NEXT. Draw arrow from PREV directly to NEXT, skipping TO_DELETE. Use dotted line to show TO_DELETE being disconnected.
Draw boxes with three sections: LEFT arrow (PREV), CENTER (DATA), RIGHT arrow (NEXT). Arrows point left for prev and right for next. Shows bidirectional traffic.
Draw boxes in a circle with arrows all pointing clockwise. The last box's arrow wraps around to point back to the first box (HEAD).

analogies.analogy: Think of an array as a row of numbered lockers (contiguous, fixed numbers) and a linked list as a treasure hunt where each clue (node) points to the next location (pointer). You must follow clues sequentially; you can't jump directly to locker 50.
Like adding a new person at the front of a ticket line. They simply stand first and everyone else shifts back one position. No one needs to move physically, just the 'first' marker changes.
A node is like a train car (carries data/passengers), and the pointer is like the coupling that connects to the next car. Each car knows only about the car behind it (in singly linked).
Like reading a book page by page. You start at page 1 (head), read it, then move to the next page using the page number/reference, continuing until you reach the last page (NULL).
Array is like a fixed number of seats on a bus. Linked list is like a ferry that can add more boats to the chain when more passengers arrive - no fixed capacity.
Think of a carousel or merry-go-round. After the last horse, you naturally loop back to the first horse. There is no 'end' - it's a continuous circle.
Like a双向地铁列车 (bidirectional subway train). Each car has connections to both the car in front AND the car behind, so you can travel in either direction along the train.
